food_name: Beef Burger (Fast Food Style, Double Patty, Sesame Bun, Cheese)
food_subgroup: Fast food beef burger — two thin beef patties on a sesame seed bun with processed cheese, shredded iceberg lettuce, tomato slices, pickles, and special sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for a fast food double-patty beef burger. Closest parent is a beef sandwich or burger food product. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fast food beef burger — double beef patty, sesame bun, processed cheese, lettuce, tomato, special sauce (Singapore fast food context)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BDF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a beef burger composite food. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for the double-patty fast food format.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to a fast food double-patty beef burger.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard fast food double-patty beef burger weighing approximately 200g, comprising two beef patties, sesame seed bun, processed cheese, lettuce, tomato, pickles, and special sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Burger (Fast Food) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fast food beef burger. All per-100g values are taken from this verified source. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods under Project Veritas v2.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Hamburger / Beef Burger — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~70 (high) is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for beef burger / hamburger on a white sesame seed bun. The GI is driven entirely by the white wheat bun (GI ~70–75); the beef patties and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate and have no GI effect. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 45) / 100 = 31.5. This is consistent with the high-GL classification for a standard fast food double-patty burger.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 495 mg per 100g; a 200g burger delivers approximately 990 mg sodium, nearly half the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should significantly limit or avoid fast food beef burgers; requesting no cheese and no pickles can reduce sodium by approximately 200–300 mg per serving, but the food remains high-sodium overall.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL 31.5) driven by the white sesame seed bun at GI ~70, delivering approximately 45g carbohydrate per 200g burger
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit fast food beef burgers due to the high glycemic load; removing the top bun reduces carbohydrate by approximately 15g and meaningfully lowers GL, while the beef protein (14.5g/100g) provides some post-prandial glycaemic moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of saturated fat (5.2g/100g), trans fat (0.3g/100g from commercial beef frying), high sodium (495mg/100g), and cholesterol (52mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit fast food beef burgers; the trans fat content activates proinflammatory NF-κB pathways, raises LDL, and lowers HDL, compounding the cardiovascular risk from saturated fat and high sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.3g/100g) is strongly proinflammatory; high sodium further promotes inflammatory burden
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit fast food beef burgers; the trans fat from commercial cooking is the primary concern, though the beef's zinc (3.5mg/100g) and selenium (22mcg/100g) provide partial anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The fast food beef burger — in Singapore primarily associated with McDonald's (Big Mac, Quarter Pounder), Burger King (Whopper), and A&W — is one of the most globally recognised foods and among the most frequently consumed by younger Singapore residents. The double-patty format (the reference for this entry) consists of two thin beef patties pressed from minced beef, cooked on a flat grill, placed in a toasted sesame seed bun with processed American cheese, shredded iceberg lettuce, tomato slices, pickles, and a proprietary special sauce (a mayonnaise-ketchup-relish blend). McDonald's is the dominant fast food chain in Singapore with over 140 locations. The burger is soft, savoury, and rich in umami from the beef and cheese, with a slight sweetness from the sauce and bun. Nutritionally, the GI (~70, high) is driven entirely by the white wheat sesame bun — the beef patties themselves have negligible carbohydrate. The processed cheese and special sauce are the primary contributors to sodium (~990mg per 200g burger). Saturated fat (5.2g/100g) and trans fat (0.3g/100g) from commercial frying are cardiovascular concerns. Genuine nutritional benefits include zinc (3.5mg/100g), selenium (22mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.5mcg/100g), and niacin (6.5mg/100g) from the beef. Clinically, this food is high concern for hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GL 31.5 per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium). It is classified as ultra-processed and is not suitable for regular consumption in patients managing any of these conditions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for beef burger on white bun. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the verified source. No estimation was required for primary nutrient values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for fast food beef burger not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food. All other nutrient fields are fully populated from the verified HPB source. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for hamburger/beef burger on white sesame bun; the GI is driven entirely by the bun carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (70 × 45) / 100 = 31.5. The halal dietary flag reflects that McDonald's Singapore and Burger King Singapore operate under MUIS halal certification for their beef products. The alias 'McDonald's burger' is a common colloquial reference and does not imply endorsement or brand-specific nutritional data.
